ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for bubble tea with milk less sugar no topping; A0FKP (tea-based beverage with milk) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor, representing a parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bubble tea beverage category.

preparation_method: blended with non-dairy creamer or milk
sweetened with sugar syrup at reduced level

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one medium cup of approximately 500 ml (500 g), which is the typical retail size sold at bubble tea stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bubble tea with milk (less sugar, no topping) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for bubble tea with milk (less sugar, no topping), cr_id D08010009, confidence 0.95. All per_100g values preserved from the HPB canonical record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — milk tea / sweetened tea-based beverage analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bubble tea with milk less sugar no topping in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated by analogy to sweetened milk tea beverages reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) and related peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 31.15) / 100 = 21.8, based on 500 g serving with 6.23 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar syrup and non-dairy creamer with potential saturated fat; sodium is low at 12 mg/100 g but the sweetened beverage pattern and possible palm-oil-based creamer contribute cardiovascular risk relevant to hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this beverage due to its added sugar content and potential saturated fat from non-dairy creamer; request an unsweetened or zero-sugar milk tea variant to reduce metabolic load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar syrup; GI estimated at 70 (high) and glycemic load of 21.8 per 500 ml serving; even the 'less sugar' variant retains added sugar syrup making it unsuitable for blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely, as the added sugar syrup produces a high glycemic load per serving and the non-dairy creamer may contain trans fats that worsen insulin resistance; choose unsweetened tea or water instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Likely contains trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from partially hydrogenated non-dairy creamer and saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g); added sugar contributes to triglyceride elevation; no beneficial omega-3 fatty acids or dietary fibre above 3 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this beverage due to the presence of trans fat and saturated fat from non-dairy creamer and the added sugar load that elevates triglycerides; opt for plain unsweetened tea with fresh low-fat milk if a tea beverage is desired.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat from non-dairy creamer is strongly pro-inflammatory; no anti-inflammatory components (no omega-3s, no meaningful polyphenols, dietary fibre below 3 g/100 g).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the trans fat content from non-dairy creamer is pro-inflammatory and no compensating anti-inflammatory nutrients are present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubble Tea with Milk (Less Sugar, No Topping) is a freshly prepared sweetened milk tea beverage popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, originating from Taiwan. It is made by combining brewed black tea or oolong tea with non-dairy creamer or fresh milk and a reduced quantity of sugar syrup, then shaken or blended with ice. Unlike the standard version, this variant omits customisable toppings such as tapioca pearls (boba), grass jelly, or pudding, and uses a lower sugar level. The beverage has a creamy, mildly sweet tea flavour with a smooth texture. Per 100 g, it provides 49 kcal, 0.4 g protein, 2.41 g fat (including approximately 1.8 g saturated fat and an estimated 0.1 g trans fat from non-dairy creamer), 6.23 g carbohydrate, 4.82 g sugar, 1.39 g dietary fibre, and 12 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high) by analogy to sweetened milk tea beverages, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 21.8 per standard 500 ml serving. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, this remains a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with added sugar syrup and is clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The non-dairy creamer component may contain partially hydrogenated vegetable oils contributing trans fat, which is pro-inflammatory and atherogenic. It is widely sold at hawker centres, bubble tea chains, and food courts across Singapore. The HPB Singapore Foodo database lists this item under Beverages > Speciality tea with cr_id D08010009.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id D08010009) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) as no direct measurement exists for this specific variant; this is documented. Multilingual names are independently verified. Overall confidence is high for macronutrients and moderate for GI, yielding a composite high confidence rating.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this bubble tea variant is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010009) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.1 g/100 g based on the input health_suitability caution noting 'Trans fat 0.1g/100g' from non-dairy creamer; this is an estimated value consistent with typical non-dairy creamer formulations and is documented as an estimate. 3. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on typical non-dairy creamer and milk tea composition; not explicitly reported in HPB source. 4. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrient fields are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this item as of 2026-04-16. 5. water_g is set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. 6. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature; no direct GI measurement for bubble tea with milk less sugar no topping exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (70 × 6.23 × 5) / 100 = 21.8 for a 500 g serving. 7. Dietary fibre of 1.39 g/100 g is preserved from HPB source; this is unusually high for a liquid beverage and may reflect measurement methodology or inclusion of soluble fibre from tea solids. 8. The 'less sugar' designation reduces but does not eliminate added sugar; this beverage remains classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) for clinical purposes. 9. FoodEx2 code A0FKP is used as the closest defensible analogous anchor; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for bubble tea with milk.
